Party_Details.Name: Ng Heng Liat

Facts.Text: Ng Heng Liat was a director of Aquagen International Pte Ltd (AIPL) during a period of project development.
Disagreements arose between the board of directors and other project participants regarding whether to continue a desalination plant project using a concrete or steel evaporator.
Independent legal advice recommended that the company participate in arbitration proceedings relating to the project disputes.
Ng Heng Liat and other directors objected to the company's participation in the arbitration.
Repeated attempts to convene a quorate board meeting to resolve the arbitration issue failed as the directors, including Ng Heng Liat, did not confirm their attendance.
A minority shareholder issued a statutory notice under section 216A of the Companies Act, demanding the company sue the directors for alleged breaches of fiduciary duty.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the conduct of a director in blocking board-level decisions regarding arbitration exposes them to a direct claim by a minority shareholder, rather than a claim brought by the company itself.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the proper plaintiff rule, a wrong done to a company must be redressed by the company itself. Shareholders wishing to sue on the company's behalf must first obtain court leave under statutory mechanisms such as section 216A.
Party_Details.Application: The directors blocked the company's participation in arbitration and prevented a quorate board meeting from taking place. A minority shareholder attempted to challenge this conduct by seeking a legal declaration in its own name. The validity of this challenge depends on whether the shareholder bypassed the mandatory statutory requirements for a derivative action.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge held that the proper plaintiff in respect of a wrong done to a company is the company itself. The court found that statutory provisions in sections 216 and 216A do not abrogate the proper plaintiff rule but provide a specific path for shareholders that requires court leave. Because the minority shareholder had already recognized this by issuing a section 216A notice, it could not then attempt to seek the same relief through a direct counterclaim in its own name without obtaining the required leave. Allowing such a claim would bypass statutory safeguards requiring proof of good faith and the company's best interests.

Party_Details.Name: Hey Bong koi

Facts.Text: Hey Bong koi was a director of Aquagen International Pte Ltd (AIPL).
Following a dispute over technical changes to a desalination project, Hey Bong koi opposed the company's entry into arbitration.
Hey Bong koi did not confirm attendance at board meetings intended to resolve the arbitration strategy, leading to a lack of quorum.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director's decision to withhold attendance from board meetings constitutes a corporate wrong that a shareholder can sue for directly.
Party_Details.Rule: Company law requires that actions for wrongs done to a company be brought by the company unless a shareholder satisfies the conditions for a derivative action under section 216A.
Party_Details.Application: A director participated in a deadlock that prevented the company from following legal advice to arbitrate a project dispute. A shareholder sought to hold the director liable for this inaction without following the statutory procedure for representing the company's interests.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court applied the rule in Foss v Harbottle, maintaining that the company is the correct entity to seek redress for breaches of directorial duty. It ruled that procedural rules for counterclaims do not grant standing to a shareholder where they otherwise lack the right to sue. As the shareholder had not obtained leave to act for the company, the claim against the director could not proceed.

Party_Details.Name: Lau Kim Yang

Facts.Text: Lau Kim Yang served as a director of Aquagen International Pte Ltd during a period of project-related conflict.
Lau Kim Yang was among the directors who did not facilitate a board resolution regarding the company's participation in desalination-related arbitration.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a minority shareholder has standing to seek a declaration of breach of duty against a director who contributes to a board deadlock.
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder may only pursue a claim belonging to the company if they obtain the court's permission under the statutory derivative action framework.
Party_Details.Application: The director's refusal to attend board meetings caused an operational deadlock. A shareholder attempted to use this deadlock as a basis for a lawsuit in their own name, bypassing the gatekeeping function of section 216A of the Companies Act.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court struck out the claim against the director because the shareholder had not secured court leave. The judge emphasized that statutory derivative actions exist precisely to ensure that such suits are brought in good faith and in the company's interests, and cannot be circumvented by individual shareholder actions.

Party_Details.Name: Kiyue Co Ltd

Facts.Text: Kiyue Co Ltd was a minority shareholder of Aquagen International Pte Ltd (AIPL).
Kiyue supported the company’s participation in arbitration to resolve project difficulties, but was blocked by the company's directors.
Kiyue issued a formal notice to the company's directors under section 216A, indicating its intent to seek court leave to sue the directors on behalf of the company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a minority shareholder can pursue a legal remedy for a wrong done to the company by bringing a claim in its own name.
Party_Details.Rule: The proper plaintiff rule prevents individual shareholders from suing for wrongs to the company unless they are granted leave to bring a statutory derivative action.
Party_Details.Application: Kiyue was a minority shareholder concerned about board deadlock. Despite issuing a statutory notice of its intent to sue on the company's behalf, it attempted to seek a judicial declaration against the directors in its own capacity as a shareholder.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court ruled that Kiyue lacked the standing (locus standi) to bring the claim. Because the alleged wrong—blocking the company's arbitration—was a wrong to the company itself, Kiyue was required to obtain the court's permission under section 216A. The court found that Kiyue's attempt to sue in its own name was an attempt to bypass the legal requirement to prove that such an action was in the best interests of the company.

Party_Details.Name: Aquagen International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Aquagen International Pte Ltd was a company involved in a desalination project that became stalled due to technical and board-level disputes.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the company is the only party entitled to seek relief for wrongs committed by its directors.
Party_Details.Rule: The company is the proper plaintiff for actions involving breaches of directorial duties.
Party_Details.Application: The company faced internal deadlock regarding its legal strategy in a desalination project. A shareholder and the directors disputed whether the company should be the formal claimant in subsequent legal actions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court affirmed that the company is the entity to which directors owe their duties. Consequently, any claim regarding those duties must be managed through the company or via a court-authorized derivative action. The court struck out attempts by a shareholder to bypass this structure.
